items.prompt_md: While finding the minimum of a rotated sorted array, you see `nums[mid] > nums[hi]`. What does that tell you?
options: The rotation point (minimum) is to the right of mid, so `lo = mid + 1`
The array isn't actually rotated after all
The rotation point (minimum) is at or to the left of mid, so `hi = mid`
`mid` itself must be the minimum
items.answer: The rotation point (minimum) is to the right of mid, so `lo = mid + 1`
items.explanation_md: If `nums[mid] > nums[hi]`, the sequence from `mid` to `hi` must contain a drop — that drop is the rotation point — so the minimum lies strictly after `mid`.

items.prompt_md: In the same search, what does `nums[mid] <= nums[hi]` tell you, and why does the code respond with `hi = mid` rather than `hi = mid - 1`?
options: `mid..hi` is already sorted increasing, so the minimum is at `mid` or somewhere to its left — and `mid` itself might be that minimum, so it can't be excluded from the range
It means the array has no rotation point at all, so the search can stop immediately
It means `mid` is definitely the rotation point, so the function should return `nums[mid]` right away
It means the right half must be discarded entirely, including `hi`
items.answer: `mid..hi` is already sorted increasing, so the minimum is at `mid` or somewhere to its left — and `mid` itself might be that minimum, so it can't be excluded from the range
items.explanation_md: Since `mid..hi` climbing means no drop-off happened in that half, the minimum must be at `mid` or earlier — and because `mid` is still a live candidate, `hi` shrinks to `mid` instead of past it.

items.prompt_md: Why does this technique compare `nums[mid]` against `nums[hi]` specifically, rather than against `nums[lo]`?
options: Comparing to `nums[hi]` cleanly distinguishes the two cases (drop-off in the right half vs. right half still climbing) even when `mid` and `lo` happen to be equal in value; comparing to `nums[lo]` can be ambiguous in that situation
It doesn't matter — comparing to `nums[lo]` gives identical results in every case
Because `nums[hi]` is always the smallest value in the array
Because `nums[lo]` is never initialized before the loop starts
items.answer: Comparing to `nums[hi]` cleanly distinguishes the two cases (drop-off in the right half vs. right half still climbing) even when `mid` and `lo` happen to be equal in value; comparing to `nums[lo]` can be ambiguous in that situation
items.explanation_md: Anchoring the comparison at the right end of the current range gives an unambiguous signal for which half contains the rotation point across the shrinking range.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of finding the minimum in a rotated sorted array of length n using the halving approach?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(log n)
items.explanation_md: Each comparison against an endpoint discards half of the remaining range, so the search converges in about log2(n) steps, the same halving guarantee as ordinary binary search.

items.prompt_md: How does the rotated-array minimum search compare to scanning the whole array once to track the smallest value seen?
options: The halving search is O(log n) versus O(n) for a full linear scan
Both approaches are O(n), but the halving search uses less extra memory
The halving search is O(n) and the linear scan is O(log n)
Both approaches are O(log n) once rotation is accounted for
items.answer: The halving search is O(log n) versus O(n) for a full linear scan
items.explanation_md: A linear scan has no way to skip anything since it doesn't know where the rotation happened, while comparing against an endpoint at `mid` lets half the range be discarded every step.

items.prompt_md: Treating a row-sorted 2-D matrix (where each row continues where the previous one ended) as one flattened sorted array, how do you convert a flattened index `mid` back into a `(row, col)` pair?
options: `row = mid // cols`, `col = mid % cols`
`row = mid % cols`, `col = mid // cols`
`row = mid // rows`, `col = mid % rows`
`row = mid`, `col = mid`
items.answer: `row = mid // cols`, `col = mid % cols`
items.explanation_md: Dividing by the row width gives how many full rows are skipped (the row index), and the remainder gives the position within that row (the column index) — the standard flatten-to-grid mapping.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of binary searching a row-sorted `rows x cols` matrix (treated as one flattened sorted sequence) for a target value?
options: O(log(rows * cols))
O(rows * cols)
O(rows + cols)
O(log(rows) * log(cols))
items.answer: O(log(rows * cols))
items.explanation_md: Flattening the grid gives a sorted sequence of length `rows * cols`, and binary search over a sequence of that length costs O(log(rows * cols)).

items.prompt_md: In a rotated array, what makes it briefly impossible to tell which half is sorted when `nums[mid] == nums[hi]`?
options: Equal values at `mid` and `hi` could mean either half is the sorted one, since the comparison that normally distinguishes them gives no information — this edge case usually needs `hi -= 1` to make progress safely
It means the array definitely isn't rotated at all
It means `mid` must be the minimum, so the search can return immediately
It never happens if the array has no duplicate values, so it's not actually a real edge case
items.answer: Equal values at `mid` and `hi` could mean either half is the sorted one, since the comparison that normally distinguishes them gives no information — this edge case usually needs `hi -= 1` to make progress safely
items.explanation_md: With duplicates, `nums[mid] == nums[hi]` doesn't reveal whether the drop-off is to the left or right of mid, so neither half can be safely discarded on that comparison alone — a narrower step like `hi -= 1` is needed instead.

items.prompt_md: A learner writes `hi = mid - 1` instead of `hi = mid` in the "`mid..hi` is sorted" branch of the rotated-array minimum search. What bug can this cause?
options: It can permanently exclude `mid` from the range even though `mid` might be the true minimum, causing the search to return the wrong value
It causes the loop to run forever with no possibility of terminating
It has no effect, since `mid` is never a candidate for the minimum anyway
It only matters when the array has an odd number of elements
items.answer: It can permanently exclude `mid` from the range even though `mid` might be the true minimum, causing the search to return the wrong value
items.explanation_md: Because `mid..hi` climbing means `mid` could be the minimum itself, dropping it out of the range with `mid - 1` can discard the correct answer before the search converges.

items.prompt_md: What loop invariant guarantees the rotated-array minimum search is correct?
options: The minimum element of the original sorted array is always somewhere inside `nums[lo..hi]` at the start of every iteration
`nums[mid]` always equals `nums[lo]` by the second iteration
The array becomes fully sorted again after enough iterations
`lo` and `hi` are always adjacent indices throughout the search
items.answer: The minimum element of the original sorted array is always somewhere inside `nums[lo..hi]` at the start of every iteration
items.explanation_md: Every branch only discards a half that's provably rotation-point-free, so the true minimum is always guaranteed to still be inside the shrinking range.

items.prompt_md: What is the most common cause of an infinite loop in a hand-written rotated-array minimum search?
options: Forgetting to move `lo` past `mid` (or `hi` down to `mid`) in at least one branch, so the range never shrinks
Starting `hi` at `len(nums)` instead of `len(nums) - 1`
Comparing `nums[mid]` to `nums[lo]` instead of a fixed sentinel value
Returning `nums[lo]` instead of `lo` once the loop ends
items.answer: Forgetting to move `lo` past `mid` (or `hi` down to `mid`) in at least one branch, so the range never shrinks
items.explanation_md: As with ordinary binary search, correctness and termination both depend on every branch making the range strictly smaller — skipping that update in any branch stalls the loop forever.

items.prompt_md: For an `n`-element rotated array, how does trying every index one at a time to find the minimum compare to the halving approach?
options: Linear scan is O(n) versus O(log n) for the halving approach
Both are O(log n), but linear scan uses less memory
Linear scan is O(log n) and the halving approach is O(n)
Both are O(n) since the array isn't fully sorted
items.answer: Linear scan is O(n) versus O(log n) for the halving approach
items.explanation_md: A full scan has no way to skip any element since it doesn't know where the rotation happened, while identifying the sorted half at each step lets the halving approach discard half the range every comparison.

items.prompt_md: You're given a sorted array that was rotated at an unknown pivot and must find its minimum value, faster than checking every element.
options: Linear scan tracking the smallest value seen
Binary-search the rotated array by identifying which half is sorted
Hash set of visited values
Two pointers scanning inward from both ends
items.answer: Binary-search the rotated array by identifying which half is sorted
items.explanation_md: "Sorted but rotated" is the trigger — one half of any split is still cleanly sorted, so comparing an endpoint at `mid` still lets you halve the range each step.

items.prompt_md: You're given a matrix where every row is sorted left-to-right and each row starts where the previous row's last element ended. You need to check whether a target value exists anywhere in it.
options: Hash set of all matrix values
Scan every cell in the matrix one at a time
Binary-search the matrix as one flattened sorted sequence
Two pointers, one per row and column
items.answer: Binary-search the matrix as one flattened sorted sequence
items.explanation_md: "Sorted rows stacked into a grid" behaves like one long sorted array — mapping a flattened index to (row, col) lets ordinary binary search apply directly.

items.prompt_md: You're scanning an unsorted list of integers and need to know whether any value appears more than once.
options: Binary-search the array by identifying which half is sorted
Two pointers scanning inward from both ends
Hash set of values seen so far
Scan the whole array once, sorting nothing
items.answer: Hash set of values seen so far
items.explanation_md: There's no rotation and no sorted structure to exploit at all here — a seen-set catches a repeat the moment it's scanned, which is a hashing problem, not a search-range problem.

items.prompt_md: You're given a rotated sorted array of distinct integers and need to find whether a specific target value exists, and if so, at what index.
options: Hash map of value to index
Linear scan from the start
Two pointers converging from both ends
Binary-search the rotated array by identifying which half is sorted
items.answer: Binary-search the rotated array by identifying which half is sorted
items.explanation_md: Once you know which half of the split is genuinely sorted, you can tell whether the target could be in that half (a normal sorted-range check) or must be in the other, still-broken half — halving the range either way.

items.prompt_md: You're given a matrix where each row is individually sorted left-to-right, but the rows aren't guaranteed to continue from one another (row 2 might start lower than row 1 ends). You need to find a target value.
options: Hash set of every value in the matrix
Binary-search each row independently, since each row alone is a sorted sequence
Two pointers scanning the whole matrix diagonally
Scan every cell in the matrix one at a time
items.answer: Binary-search each row independently, since each row alone is a sorted sequence
items.explanation_md: Even without the whole-grid continuity property, each individual row is still a sorted sequence, so a per-row binary search still beats scanning every cell.

items.prompt_md: You're given an unsorted list of purchase amounts and need to count how many times each distinct amount occurs.
options: Binary-search the array by identifying which half is sorted
Hash map counting occurrences of each amount
Two pointers scanning inward from both ends
Sort the array, then binary search for boundaries
items.answer: Hash map counting occurrences of each amount
items.explanation_md: Tallying frequencies over unsorted data has no rotation or sortedness to exploit — a hash map of amount to count solves it directly in one linear pass.

items.prompt_md: You're given a sorted array that was rotated some unknown number of times and need to figure out exactly how many rotations occurred.
options: Binary-search for the rotation point (the index of the minimum element)
Hash set of rotated positions
Linear scan comparing every adjacent pair
Two pointers scanning inward from both ends
items.answer: Binary-search for the rotation point (the index of the minimum element)
items.explanation_md: The number of rotations equals the index of the minimum element, and since one half of any split is always cleanly sorted, that index can be found by halving the range rather than scanning every position.

items.prompt_md: You're given a matrix that's sorted both left-to-right along every row and top-to-bottom along every column (a "staircase" matrix), and need to check whether a target value exists.
options: Hash set of every value in the matrix
Scan every cell one at a time
Two pointers scanning inward diagonally
Binary-search the matrix by exploiting the row/column sort order
items.answer: Binary-search the matrix by exploiting the row/column sort order
items.explanation_md: "Sorted rows stacked into a grid" (here along both axes) still means order can be exploited to eliminate large chunks of the matrix per comparison rather than checking every cell.

items.prompt_md: You need to search a row-sorted, row-continuing matrix for a target by first mapping a flattened index `mid` to `(mid // cols, mid % cols)`. Which approach does this mapping support?
options: Two pointers walking the rows and columns simultaneously
Hash map from flattened index to value
Binary-search the matrix as one flattened sorted sequence
Linear scan row by row
items.answer: Binary-search the matrix as one flattened sorted sequence
items.explanation_md: That row/column mapping exists precisely so a single flattened binary search can treat the whole grid as one long sorted array.

items.prompt_md: You're given a rotated sorted array that may contain duplicate values and need to determine whether a target value is present.
options: Two pointers scanning inward from both ends
Binary-search the rotated array, falling back to shrinking one endpoint when duplicates make the sorted half ambiguous
Hash set of all array values
Linear scan checking every element
items.answer: Binary-search the rotated array, falling back to shrinking one endpoint when duplicates make the sorted half ambiguous
items.explanation_md: The core rotated-array halving idea still applies; duplicates just introduce a rare tie case (`nums[mid] == nums[hi]`) that needs a small extra step, not an entirely different approach.

items.prompt_md: You're given a sorted array that was rotated at an unknown pivot and need to find its maximum value rather than its minimum.
options: Binary-search the rotated array by identifying which half is sorted
Hash set of all values, then take the max
Linear scan tracking the largest value seen
Two pointers scanning inward from both ends
items.answer: Binary-search the rotated array by identifying which half is sorted
items.explanation_md: The maximum sits right before the rotation point, and that point is still found the same way — by comparing endpoints to identify which half is cleanly sorted and halving the range.

items.prompt_md: You're given an unsorted array of integers and must return the original indices of two numbers that add up to a target sum.
options: Binary-search the array by identifying which half is sorted
Two pointers converging from both ends
Linear scan checking every pair
Hash map of value to index
items.answer: Hash map of value to index
items.explanation_md: There's no rotation or sorted structure here, and sorting would destroy the indices you need to return — a hash map of value to index finds the complement in one linear pass instead.
